class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(filename) </span> <span id="cb496-10"><a href="#cb496-10" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>pdb_format <span class="op">=</span> <span class="st">'6s5s1s4s1s3s1s1s4s1s3s8s8s8s6s6s6s4s2s3s'</span> </span> <span id="cb496-11"><a href="#cb496-11" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>A, B, result <span class="op">=</span> [], [], [] </span> <span id="cb496-12"><a href="#cb496-12" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">for</span> line <span class="kw">in</span> in_file: <span class="cf">if</span> line[<span class="dv">0</span>:<span class="dv">4</span>] <span class="op">==</span> <span class="st">"ATOM"</span>: col <span class="op">=</span> struct unpack(pdb_format, line) a_name <span class="op">=</span> col[<span class="dv">3</span>].strip() chain <span class="op">=</span> col[<span class="dv">7</span>].strip() amino_numer <span class="op">=</span> col[<span class="dv">5</span>].strip() <span class="op">+</span> col[<span class="dv">8</span>].strip() x <span class="op">=</span> col[<span class="dv">11</span>].strip() y <span class="op">=</span> col[<span class="dv">12</span>].strip() z <span class="op">=</span> col[<span class="dv">13</span>].strip() <span class="cf">if</span> a_name <span class="op">==</span> <span class="st">"CA"</span>: <span class="cf">if</span> chain <span class="op">==</span> chain1: A.append((amino_numer, x, y, z)) <span class="cf">if</span> chain <span class="op">==</span> chain2: B.append((amino_numer, x, y, z)) </span> <span id="cb496-13"><a href="#cb496-13" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="co">#calculate pairs of atoms with distance < 6 </span></span> <span id="cb496-14"><a href="#cb496-14" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">for</span> i <span class="kw">in</span> <span class="bu">range</span>(<span class="bu">len</span>(A)): <span class="cf">for</span> j <span class="kw">in</span> <span class="bu">range</span>(<span class="bu">len</span>(B)): v1 <span class="op">=</span> (<span class="bu">float</span>(A[i][<span class="dv">1</span>]), <span class="bu">float</span>(A[i][<span class="dv">2</span>]), <span class="bu">float</span>(A[i][<span class="dv">3</span>])) v2 <span class="op">=</span> (<span class="bu">float</span>(B[j][<span class="dv">1</span>]), <span class="bu">float</span>(B[j][<span class="dv">2</span>]), <span class="bu">float</span>(B[j][<span class="dv">3</span>])) tmp <span class="op">=</span> calcDist(v1, v2) <span class="cf">if</span> tmp <span class="op"><</span> <span class="dv">6</span>: result.append((A[i][<span class="dv">0</span>], B[j][<span class="dv">0</span>], tmp)) <span class="cf">return</span> result </span> <span id="cb496-15"><a href="#cb496-15" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="bu">print</span> getInterface(<span class="st">"2H8L.pdb"</span>, <span class="st">"A"</span>, <span class="st">"B"</span>) </span></code></pre></div> <p>Source: Adapted from code published by A.Via/K.Rother under the Python License.</p> </section> <section id="with-biopython" class="level1"> <h1>WITH BIOPYTHON</h1> <p>The same task can be accomplished more easily using Biopython. Here, the distance between two atoms is calculated using the “-” operator, as shown in Example 21.1.</p> <p>from Bio import PDB<br /> parser <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> PDB.PDBParser()<br /> s <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> parser.get_structure(“2H8L”,“2H8L.pdb”)<br /> first_model <span class="math inline">\(\equiv\)</span> s[0]<br /> chain_A <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> first_model[“A”]<br /> chain_B <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> first_model[“B”]<br /> for res1 in chain_A: for res2 in chain_B: d <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> res1[“CA”]-res2[“CA”] if d <= 6.0: print res1.resname,res1.get_id() [1],res2.resname,\ res2.get_id() [1],d</p> <p>Source: Adapted from code published by A.Via/K.Rother under the Python License.</p> </section> <section id="recipe-17-building-homology-models-using-modeller" class="level1"> <h1>Recipe 17: Building Homology Models Using Modeller</h1> <p>Modeller [1,2] is a package designed to build homology models of protein three-dimensional structures. It is written in Python and protein three-dimensional structures. It is written in Python and can be downloaded from http://salilab.org/modeller/download_installation. html. The package consists of a number of Python scripts that you have to modify by adding the name and location of a target-template alignment file, of a target sequence file, and of a template PDB file. Also, you can set modeling parameters and add or remove specific instructions, depending on what you want to do. Once you have downloaded Modeller, you can copy a default Modeller Python script to a working directory where you want to generate your models and then open the script with a text editor and modify it. Here, a simple example of a Modeller script that builds a three-dimensional model from a single template structure (1eq9A) and a target-template alignment (alignment.ali) is displayed and discussed:</p> <p>from modeller import *<br /> from modeller.autodel import *<br /> logverbose()<br /> env <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> environ()<br /> env.io(atom_files_directory <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> [‘.’]，‘./atom_files’]<br /> a <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> automodel(env, alnfile <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> ‘alignment.ali’, knowns <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> ‘1eq9A’, sequence <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> MyTarget_Seq’)</p> <p>a.starting_model <span class="math inline">\(= 1\)</span> a ending_model <span class="math inline">\(= 1\)</span> a.make()</p> <p>Source: Adapted from code published by A.Via/K.Rother under the Python License.</p> <p>In the first two rows, all standard modeller classes are imported, as well as the automodel class. The automodel class is needed to build a “model object.” log.verbose() makes the program produce more detailed output and environ() creates an environment (env) that is required to build models. The env.io.atom_files_directory variable is set to a list of directory path(s) where the program will look for the PDB coordinate file(s) of the template(s). The PDB file of the template must be saved in at least one of the directories specified in the env.io.atom_files_ directory variable. In the example, the first element of this list indicates the current directory (.), the second element is a directory (atom_files) that is located in the parent directory of the current one (../). In the subsequent lines, input files and parameters are set. Modeller needs to know the filename of the target-template alignment file <span class="math inline">\(( \mathtt { a l n f i l e } = \mathtt { \Omega ^ { \prime } a l i g n - }\)</span> ment.ali’), the target sequence filename (sequence <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> ‘MyTarget Seq’), and the template structure filename(s) (knowns = ‘1eq9A’). The alignment.ali file must be saved in the directory where you run the script. These files, plus the environment variable (env), are used to initialize a “model object” by creating an instance of the automodel class. At this stage, no models have been built yet.</p> <p>The a.starting_model and a.ending_model attributes of the “model object” are then set to the number of models that will be generated. They define the indices of the first and last models. In the example, only one model is generated, and therefore, both variables are set to 1. Once all parameters are set, a.make() is the method that actually does the job.</p> <p>Templates for building homology models can be identified using, for example, HHpred (http://toolkit.tuebingen.mpg.de/hhpred), which also creates target-template alignments. The HHpred alignment file format (PIR format, file extension .ali) is accepted by Modeller. Here is one example of such a file (where the omitted sequences have been replaced by three dots) for this recipe:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb497"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb497-1"><a href="#cb497-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>> P1; MyTarget_Seq </span> <span id="cb497-2"><a href="#cb497-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>sequence: MyTarget_Seq: 1: : 230: :: : 0.00: 0.00 </span> <span id="cb497-3"><a href="#cb497-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>IIGGTVDVEDGKAPYLAGLVYNNSATYCGGEEHV...NSDH* </span> <span id="cb497-4"><a href="#cb497-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>> P1; 1eq9A </span> <span id="cb497-5"><a href="#cb497-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>structureX: 1eq9A: : A: : A: Chymotrypsin; FIRE ANT, proteinase, hydrolase; HET PMS; 1.70A {Solenoc SCOP b.47.1.2:Solenopsis invicta:1.70:0.26 </span> <span id="cb497-6"><a href="#cb497-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>IVGGKDAPVGKYPQVSLRLS-GSHRCGASILD...----* </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="references" class="level1"> <h1>REFERENCES</h1> <p>[1] A. Sali and T.L. Blundell, “Comparative Protein Modelling by Satisfaction of Spatial Restraints,” Journal of Molecular Biology 234 (1993): 779–815.<br /> [2] N. Eswar, M.A. Marti-Renom, B. Webb, M.S. Madhusudhan, D. Eramian, M. Shen, U. Pieper, and A. Sali, “Comparative Protein Structure Modeling with MODELLER,” Current Protocols in Bioinformatics Supplement 15 (2006): 5.6.1–5.6.30.</p> </section> <section id="recipe-18-rna-3d-homology-modeling-with-moderna" class="level1"> <h1>Recipe 18: RNA 3D Homology Modeling with ModeRNA</h1> <p>Mlibrary for analyzing, manipulating, and modeling RNA 3D struc- tures, similar to Modeller for proteins (see Recipe 17). This recipe uses odeRNA [1] (http://iimcb.genesilico.pl/moderna/) is a Python a few of the more than 30 functions in ModeRNA to remodel a short fragment from a tRNA structure. To get started, you need to download and install ModeRNA from its web page and download the structure of Phenylalanyl-tRNA from yeast (PDBID: 1EHZ) from the Protein Data Bank (http://rcsb.org/). When in doubt, the ModeRNA website provides more detailed documentation.</p> <p>This is the code to build the model:</p> <p>from moderna import *<br /> ehz <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> load_model(‘1ehz.ent’, ‘A’)<br /> clean_structure(ehz)<br /> print get_sequence(ehz)<br /> print get(secstruc(ehz)<br /> m <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> create_model()<br /> copy(Some_residues(ehz[‘1’:‘15’], m)<br /> write_model(m, ‘1ehz_15r.ent’)<br /> temp <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> load_template(‘1ehz_15r.ent’)<br /> ali <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> load Alignment(‘>>> model sequence ACUGUGAYUA[UACCU#PG<br /> > template: first 15 bases from lehz GCGGA–UUUALCUCAG’’’)</p> <p>model <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> create_model(temp,ali) print get_sequence(model)</p> <p>Source: Adapted from code published by A.Via/K.Rother under the Python License.</p> <p>When you execute this script, the following output is generated (within 1–2 minutes):</p> <p>GCGGAUUUACUCAGDDGGGAGAGCRCCAGABU#AAYAP?UGGAG7UC?UGUGTPCG”UCC ACAGAAUUCGCACCA <span class="math inline">\((\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\left(\dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots \dots .\right)\right)\right)\right)\right)\right)\right)\right)\right)\)</span> ）…. ACUGUGAYUA [UACCU#PG</p> <p>In the following, the program is explained line by line.</p> <p>The import statement in the first line imports the main functions of ModeRNA, which are used in the following commands. Then, the tRNA structure is loaded into Python. The load_model() command reads chain A from the RNA structure file ‘1ehz.ent’ and saves it in the variable ehz. The object returned by the load_model() function provides a simplified version of the Bio.PDB library that can be thus addressed in a shorter way. The clean_structure() function removes from the structure water molecules, ions, and some other problematic atoms that would conflict with the modeling, while get_sequence() returns the RNA sequence. Note that the printed sequence contains modified bases, indicated by characters other than ACGU; get_secstruc() returns base pairs in dot-bracket notation (see Recipe 8).</p> <p>In the next paragraph of the program, the first 15 residues are extracted from the RNA chain and written to a separate file. This is achieved using three functions: create_model(), copy_some_residues(), and write_model(). The create_model() function creates an empty RNA model. Next, the residues numbered from 1 to 15 are copied to that model and finally written to a file by the write_model() function.</p> <p>To build a model, similar to Modeller (see Recipe 17), ModeRNA needs two things: the template structure and a target-template pairwise sequence alignment. As a template, the 15-residue file created in the previous step is used. It is loaded by the load_template() function. The target-template alignment is given as a FASTA string to the load_alignment() function, but reading the alignment from a file would work as well. In this case, the argument of the load_alignment() function will be the alignment</p> <p>filename. Note that the second sequence in the alignment must be identical to the sequence of the template structure (you can use the get_ sequence() function to verify that). Furthermore, the two sequences in the alignment must have exactly the same length.</p> <p>Finally, the create_model() command starts the automatic homology modeling procedure in ModeRNA: bases are exchanged and modifications are added as specified in the alignment. Where there are gaps, ModeRNA attempts to find a fitting piece of RNA structure in its internal library and inserts it to connect the residues from the template (the A and U at the borders of the gap, in this case). After that you can write the model to a file with write_model() or further work on it in Python. As you can see from the output, the sequence of the model is identical to the one of the target from the alignment.</p> <p>Although the Python commands to build RNA models may look easy, obtaining high quality models is complicated. The template structure needs to be chosen carefully, the alignment needs to be curated manually, and the resulting model may need further refinement. The ModeRNA library allows you to perform modeling of RNA structures step by step, and therefore facilitates work with RNA 3D structures in general.</p> </section> <section id="reference-2" class="level1"> <h1>REFERENCE</h1> <p>[1] M. Rother, K. Rother, T. Puton, and J.M. Bujnicki, “ModeRNA: A Tool for Comparative Modeling of RNA 3D Structure,” Nucleic Acids Research 39 (2011): 4007–4022.</p> </section> <section id="recipe-19-calculating-rna-base-pairs-from-a-3d-structure" class="level1"> <h1>Recipe 19: Calculating RNA Base Pairs from a 3D Structure</h1> <p>When analyzing an RNA 3D structure, one of the first things getting attention are the base pairs. Key interactions in RNA are not only attention are the base pairs. Key interactions in RNA are not only canonical Watson-Crick pairs but also many noncanonical base pairs. This recipe explains how to calculate the base pairs from Python using PyCogent.</p> <p>There are several programs that can calculate base pairs from the PDB structure of an RNA molecule: for a long time, RNAView has set an unchallenged standard. The RNAView software (http://ndbserver.rutgers. edu/services/download) identifies all 12 types of noncanonical base pairs defined by both Leontis and Westhof [1, 2]. The program can be compiled on Linux. Afterwards, all base pairs (e.g., the PDB file 1ehz.pdb) can be calculated with the Linux shell command:</p> <p>rnaview 1ehz.pdb</p> <p>RNAView creates a couple of text files as an output. The file basepairs.out contains a list of base pairing interactions in tabular format:</p> <table> <tr> <td> 1_72, A: </td> <td> 1 G-C 72 </td> <td> A: +/- cis </td> <td> XIX </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 2_71, A: </td> <td> 2 C-G 71 </td> <td> A: +/- cis </td> <td> XIX </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 3_70, A: </td> <td> 3 G-C 70 </td> <td> A: +/- cis </td> <td> XIX </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 4_69, A: </td> <td> 4 G-C 69 </td> <td> A: +/- cis </td> <td> XIX </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 5_68, A: </td> <td> 5 G-C 68 </td> <td> A: +/- cis </td> <td> XIX </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 6_67, A: </td> <td> 6 A-U 67 </td> <td> A: W/W cis </td> <td> n/a </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7_66, A: </td> <td> 7 A-U 66 </td> <td> A: -/- cis </td> <td> XX </td> </tr> </table> <p>The PyCogent library [3] (see Recipe 1) contains a parser that allows you to parse these files:</p> <p>from cogent.app.rnview import RnaView<br /> from cogent数据分析.rnview import RnviewParser<br /> rna_prog = RnaView()<br /> result <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> rna_prog(‘1ehz.pdb’)<br /> bpairs <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> result[‘base_pairs’]<br /> errors <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> result[‘StdErr’].read()<br /> stdout <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> result[‘StdOut’].read()<br /> bp_dict <span class="math inline">\(\equiv\)</span> RnviewParser(bpairs)<br /> print(‘INFORMATION:’)<br /> sys/stderr.write errors)<br /> print(stdout) print’BASE PAIRS:’<br /> for key in bp_dict: print key, bp_dict[key]</p> <p>The program consists of two parts. First, the cogent.app.rnaview module is used to execute the RNAView command line tool on a PDB file. The rnaview module contains a program wrapper RnaView (see Chapter 14), which returns a dictionary with separate entries for the standard output (messages on the screen), errors, and the base pair output. In the second step, a parser for the base pair format from the module cogent. parse.rnaview is used. The RnaviewParser parser produces a dictionary with the residue numbers as keys and various information, including the base pair type, as values.</p> </section> <section id="alternatives-to-rnaview" class="level1"> <h1>ALTERNATIVES TO RNAVIEW</h1> <p>A few interesting, more recent alternatives to RNAView exist. The FR3D software (http://rna.bgsu.edu/FR3D) can identify all kinds of base pairs but requires the commercial MATLAB package. Without MATLAB, FR3D can be used via a web interface (http://rna.bgsu.edu/WebFR3D). The website also provides a huge catalog of exemplary base pair structures that count as a gold standard (http://rna.bgsu.edu/FR3D/basepairs). RNAView is a no-cost alternative, which is why we selected it for this recipe. Base pairs can also be calculated online using the MC-Annotate tool [4] (www-lbit.iro.umontreal.ca/mcannotate-simple). Finally, to calculate Watson-Crick base pairs, you can use the ModeRNA software [5] (http://iimcb.genesilico.pl/moderna; see Recipe 18), writing the following fragment of Python code:</p> <p>from moderna import * struc <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> load_model(‘1ehz.pdb’, ‘A’) for bp in get_base_pairs(struc): print(bp) ModeRNA also calculates noncanonical pairs, but at the time of writing, this feature is in a development stage. Keep in mind that each program calculates base pairs in a slightly different way, and therefore the resulting base pairs are not <span class="math inline">\(1 0 0 \%\)</span> identical.</p> </section> <section id="references-1" class="level1"> <h1>REFERENCES</h1> <p>[1] M. Sarver, C.L. Zirbel, J. Stombaugh, A. Mokdad, and N.B. Leontis, “FR3D: Finding Local and Composite Recurrent Structural Motifs in RNA 3D Structures,” Journal of Mathematical Biology 56 (2008): 215–252.<br /> [2] H. Yang, F. Jossinet, N. Leontis, L. Chen, J. Westbrook, H. Berman, and E. Westhof, “Tools for the Automatic Identification and Classification of RNA Base Pairs,” Nucleic Acids Research 31 (2003): 3450–3460.<br /> [3] R. Knight, P. Maxwell, A. Birmingham, J. Carnes, J.G. Caporaso, B.C. Easton, M. Eaton, M. Hamady, H. Lindsay, Z. Liu, C. Lozupone, D. McDonald, M. Robeson, R. Sammut, S. Smit, M.J. Wakefield, J. Widmann, S. Wikman, S. Wilson, H. Ying, and G.A. Huttley, “PyCogent: A Toolkit for Making Sense from Sequence,” Genome Biology 8 (2007): R171.<br /> [4] P. Gendron, S. Lemieux, and F. Major, “Quantitative Analysis of Nucleic Acid Three-Dimensional Structures,” Journal of Molecular Biology 308 (2001): 919–936.<br /> [5] M. Rother, K. Rother, T. Puton, and J.M. Bujnicki, “ModeRNA: A Tool for Comparative Modeling of RNA 3D Structure,” Nucleic Acids Research 39 (2011): 4007–4022.</p> </section> <section id="recipe-20-a-real-case-of-structural-superimposition-the-serine-protease-catalytic-triad" class="level1"> <h1>Recipe 20: A Real Case of Structural Superimposition: The Serine Protease Catalytic Triad</h1> <p>Serine proteases are enzymes cleaving peptide bonds in proteins. The catalysis occurs in the active site of the enzyme, which is made up of three residues very close in space: a histidine (His57), an aspartic acid (Asp102), and a serine (Ser195). The residue numbers of the three catalytic residues are Hist57, Asp102, and Ser195 in almost all serine protease structures. Ser195 serves as the nucleophilic amino acid. It has been observed that the active site triad is structurally very well conserved; i.e., the relative position of the residues homologous to His57, Asp102, and Ser195 in three-dimensional (3D) space is very well conserved in even globally different serine proteases. In most cases, the catalytic residues are not close in sequence. Serine proteases are classified in two categories (chymotrypsin/trypsin-like and subtilisin-like) depending on the global fold of the enzymes.</p> <p>In this example, Biopython is used to superimpose the structures of two completely different serine proteases on the backbone atoms (CA and N) of the amino acids of the catalytic triad (for the use of Biopython to superimpose structures, see also Chapter 21, Example 21.3).</p> <p>The first structure (PDB 1EQ9) is a chymotrypsin from Solenopsis invicta, the South American imported fire ant, and the second (PDB 1FXY) is a chimeric protein obtained by recombining the N-terminal subdomain from the human coagulation factor X with the C-terminal subdomain from a trypsin. By superimposing the two structures onto the His57, Asp102, and Ser195 residues, you can examine whether the catalytic triad is still well conserved between these two enzymes despite their overall differences.</p> <p>In a structural superimposition one of the two structures (the target) is kept in a fixed position, and the other (the probe) is rotated and translated until the RMSD between the two structures reaches a minimum. A superimposition must be carried out between sets of atoms of equal size. Therefore, before superimposing two structures, you must decide which atoms of each structure you want to superimpose. These could even be all the atoms present in the structure. In this case, you have to check if the two structures have the same number of atoms and if each atom in one structure has a corresponding atom in the other structure. In general, you may obtain very good results by superimposing a small number of atoms. In particular, if you want to study a specific region or domain of a protein, e.g., a binding site, it will be sufficient to superimpose the atoms of the residues belonging to the binding site. In this case, the RMSD between corresponding binding site atoms in the two structures will be calculated and minimized.</p> <p>In this example, the structure 1FXY (probe) is superimposed onto the structure 1EQ9 (target). The superimposition produces a rotation-translation matrix, which can be used to generate a rotated and translated structure out of 1FXY (1FXY-superimposed.pdb).</p> <p>At the beginning of the program, the two structures are retrieved from the PDB, i.e. downloaded from the PDB Web repository. Next, they are read into Biopython structure objects (struct_1 and struct_2). Then, the residues of the two catalytic triads are recorded in six variables in the form of residue objects. Notice that the second argument of the get_ structure() method is the path to the PDB files downloaded from the PDB. Indeed, the retrieve_pdb_file() method creates directories (eq for 1eq9.ent and fx for 1fxy.ent) where it saves the downloaded file.</p> <p>For each triad, backbone atom objects are extracted from the residue objects and appended to a list. The first list is called target because it contains the atoms that will be kept fixed during the superimposition (extracted from 1EQ9), the second is called probe and contains atoms</p> <p>that will be moved in the coordinate system during the rotation-translation (extracted from 1FXY). target and probe are used to calculate the rotation-translation matrix that will be applied to all atoms (struct_2_ atoms) of the 1FXY structure (struct_2). Finally, the rotated and translated structure (1FXY-superimposed.pdb) is saved to a file.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb498"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb498-1"><a href="#cb498-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>Superimpose the catalytic triads of two different serine </span> <span id="cb498-2"><a href="#cb498-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a># proteases (on CA and N atoms of res H57, D102, and S195 of chain A) </span></code></pre></div> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb499"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb499-1"><a href="#cb499-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>from Bio import PDB </span></code></pre></div> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb500"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb500-1"><a href="#cb500-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>Retrieve PDB files </span> <span id="cb500-2"><a href="#cb500-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>pdbl = PDB.PDBList() </span> <span id="cb500-3"><a href="#cb500-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>pdbl.retrieve_pdb_file("1EQ9") </span> <span id="cb500-4"><a href="#cb500-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>pdbl.retrieve_pdb_file("1FXY") </span></code></pre></div> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb501"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb501-1"><a href="#cb501-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="co"># Parse the two structures</span></span> <span id="cb501-2"><a href="#cb501-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> Bio.PDB <span class="im">import</span> PDBParser, Superimposer, PDBIO</span> <span id="cb501-3"><a href="#cb501-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>parser <span class="op">=</span> PDB.PDBParser()</span> <span id="cb501-4"><a href="#cb501-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>struct_1 <span class="op">=</span> parser.get_structure(<span class="st">"1EQ9"</span>, <span class="st">"eq/pdb1eq9.ent"</span>)</span> <span id="cb501-5"><a href="#cb501-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>struct_2 <span class="op">=</span> parser.get結構(<span class="st">"1FXY"</span>, <span class="st">"fx/pdb1fxy.ent"</span>) </span></code></pre></div> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb502"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb502-1"><a href="#cb502-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>get the catalytic triads </span> <span id="cb502-2"><a href="#cb502-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>res_57_struct_1 <span class="op">=</span> struct_1[<span class="dv">0</span>] [<span class="st">'A'</span>] [<span class="dv">57</span>] </span> <span id="cb502-3"><a href="#cb502-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>res_102_struct_1 <span class="op">=</span> struct_1[<span class="dv">0</span>] [<span class="st">'A'</span>] [<span class="dv">102</span>] </span> <span id="cb502-4"><a href="#cb502-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>res_195_struct_1 <span class="op">=</span> struct_1[<span class="dv">0</span>] [<span class="st">'A'</span>] [<span class="dv">195</span>] </span></code></pre></div> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb503"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb503-1"><a href="#cb503-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>res_57_struct_2 <span class="op">=</span> struct_2[<span class="dv">0</span>] [<span class="st">'A'</span>] [<span class="dv">57</span>] </span> <span id="cb503-2"><a href="#cb503-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>res_102_struct_2 <span class="op">=</span> struct_2[<span class="dv">0</span>] [<span class="st">'A'</span>] [<span class="dv">102</span>] </span> <span id="cb503-3"><a href="#cb503-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>res_195_struct_2 <span class="op">=</span> struct_2[<span class="dv">0</span>] [<span class="st">'A'</span>] [<span class="dv">195</span>] </span></code></pre></div> <p>Build 2 lists of atoms for calculating a rot.-trans. matrix # (target and probe).<br /> target <span class="math inline">\(= []\)</span> backbone_names <span class="math inline">\(\equiv\)</span> [‘CA’, ‘N’]<br /> for name in backbone_names: target.append(res_57_struct_1[name]) target.append(res_102_struct_1[name]) target.append(res_195_struct_1[name])</p> <p>probe <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> []<br /> for name in backbone_names: probe.append(res_57_struct_2[name]) probe.append(res_102_struct_2[name]) probe.append(res_195_struct_2[name])</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb504"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb504-1"><a href="#cb504-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>Check whether target <span class="kw">and</span> probe lists are equal <span class="kw">in</span> size. </span> <span id="cb504-2"><a href="#cb504-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="co"># This is needed for calculating a rot.-trans. matrix </span></span> <span id="cb504-3"><a href="#cb504-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">assert</span> <span class="bu">len</span>(target) <span class="op">==</span> <span class="bu">len</span>(probe) </span></code></pre></div> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb505"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb505-1"><a href="#cb505-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>Calculate the rotation-translation matrix. </span> <span id="cb505-2"><a href="#cb505-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>sup = Superimposer() </span></code></pre></div> <p>sup.setisablems(target,probe)<br /> #Apply the matrix. Remember that it can be applied only on #lists of atoms. struct_2isablems <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> [at for at in struct_2.getisablems()] sup.apply(struct_2isablems)<br /> #Write the rotation-translated structure out <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> PDBIO() out.set_structure(struct_2) out.save(‘1FXY-superimposed.pdb’)</p> <p>Source: Adapted from code published by A.Via/K.Rother under the Python License.</p> <p>Now you can open 1EQ9.pdb and 1FXY-superimposed.pdb with PyMOL (see Chapter 17) and verify whether the catalytic residues in the two structures are well conserved (i.e., if they are well superimposed).</p> </section> <section id="appendix-a-command-overview" class="level1"> <h1>Appendix A: Command Overview</h1> </section> <section id="a.1-unix-commands" class="level1"> <h1>A.1 UNIX COMMANDS</h1> </section> <section id="a.1.1-listing-files-and-directories" class="level1"> <h1>A.1.1 Listing Files and Directories</h1> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb506"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb506-1"><a href="#cb506-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>ls Lists files and directories </span> <span id="cb506-2"><a href="#cb506-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>ls -a Lists hidden files and directories </span> <span id="cb506-3"><a href="#cb506-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>mkdir Creates a directory </span> <span id="cb506-4"><a href="#cb506-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>cd directory Changes to named directory </span> <span id="cb506-5"><a href="#cb506-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>cd Changes to home directory </span> <span id="cb506-6"><a href="#cb506-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>cd ~ Changes to home directory </span> <span id="cb506-7"><a href="#cb506-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>cd.. Changes to parent directory </span> <span id="cb506-8"><a href="#cb506-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>pwd Displays the path of the current directory </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.1.2-handling-files-and-directories" class="level1"> <h1>A.1.2 Handling Files and Directories</h1> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb507"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb507-1"><a href="#cb507-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>cp file1 file2 Copies file1 and calls it file2 </span> <span id="cb507-2"><a href="#cb507-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>mv file1 file2 Moves or renames file1 to file2 </span> <span id="cb507-3"><a href="#cb507-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>rm file Removes a file </span> <span id="cb507-4"><a href="#cb507-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>rmdir directory Removes a directory </span> <span id="cb507-5"><a href="#cb507-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>cat file Displays a file </span> <span id="cb507-6"><a href="#cb507-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>more file Displays a file a page at a time </span> <span id="cb507-7"><a href="#cb507-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>head file Displays the first few lines of a file </span> <span id="cb507-8"><a href="#cb507-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>tail file Displays the last few lines of a file </span> <span id="cb507-9"><a href="#cb507-9" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>grep 'keyword' file Searches a file for keywords </span> <span id="cb507-10"><a href="#cb507-10" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>wc file Counts the number of lines, words, and characters in a file </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.1.3-redirection" class="level1"> <h1>A.1.3 Redirection</h1> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb508"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb508-1"><a href="#cb508-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>command > file Redirects standard output to a file </span> <span id="cb508-2"><a href="#cb508-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>command >> file Appends standard output to a file </span> <span id="cb508-3"><a href="#cb508-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>command < file Redirects standard input from a file </span> <span id="cb508-4"><a href="#cb508-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>cat file1 file2 > file0 Concatenates file1 and file2 to file0 </span> <span id="cb508-5"><a href="#cb508-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>sort Sorts data </span> <span id="cb508-6"><a href="#cb508-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>who List users currently logged in </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.1.4-file-system-security-access-rights" class="level1"> <h1>A.1.4 File System Security (Access Rights)</h1> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb509"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb509-1"><a href="#cb509-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>ls -lag Lists access rights for all files </span> <span id="cb509-2"><a href="#cb509-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>chmod [options] file Changes access rights for named file </span> <span id="cb509-3"><a href="#cb509-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>command & Runs command in background </span> <span id="cb509-4"><a href="#cb509-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>^c Killed the job running in the foreground </span> <span id="cb509-5"><a href="#cb509-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>^z Suspend the job running in the foreground </span> <span id="cb509-6"><a href="#cb509-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>bg Backgrounds the suspended job </span> <span id="cb509-7"><a href="#cb509-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>jobs Lists current jobs </span> <span id="cb509-8"><a href="#cb509-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>fg%1 Foreground job number 1 </span> <span id="cb509-9"><a href="#cb509-9" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>kill%1 Kills job number 1 </span> <span id="cb509-10"><a href="#cb509-10" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>ps Lists current processes </span> <span id="cb509-11"><a href="#cb509-11" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>kill 26152 Kills process number 26152 </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.1.5-chmod-options" class="level1"> <h1>A.1.5 chmod Options</h1> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb510"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb510-1"><a href="#cb510-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>Symbol Meaning </span> <span id="cb510-2"><a href="#cb510-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>u User </span> <span id="cb510-3"><a href="#cb510-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>g Group </span> <span id="cb510-4"><a href="#cb510-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>o Other </span> <span id="cb510-5"><a href="#cb510-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>a All </span> <span id="cb510-6"><a href="#cb510-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>r Read </span> <span id="cb510-7"><a href="#cb510-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>w Write (and delete) </span> <span id="cb510-8"><a href="#cb510-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>x Execute (and access directory) </span> <span id="cb510-9"><a href="#cb510-9" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>+ Add permission </span> <span id="cb510-10"><a href="#cb510-10" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>- Take away permission </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.1.6-general-rules" class="level1"> <h1>A.1.6 General Rules</h1> <ul> <li><p>If you’ve made a typo, use Ctrl-U to cancel the whole line.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>UNIX is case-sensitive.</p></li> <li><p>There are commands that can take options.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>The options change the behavior of the command.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>UNIX uses command-line completion.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>%command_name -options <file> [Return]<br /> </p></li> <li><p>%man <command name> [Enter]<br /> </p></li> <li><p>%whatis <command name> [Enter]<br /> </p></li> <li><p>Ctrl-A sets the cursor at the beginning of the line.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>Ctrl-E sets the cursor at the end of the line.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>You can use up and down arrows to recall commands.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>The command whereis tells you where a given program is.<br /> </p></li> <li><p>You can use a text editor to write stuff (e.g., gedit).</p></li> </ul> </section> <section id="a.2-python-commands" class="level1"> <h1>A.2 PYTHON COMMANDS</h1> </section> <section id="a.2.1-overview" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.1 Overview</h1> <ul> <li>Python is an interpreted language.<br /> </li> <li>The Python interpreter automatically generates bytecode (.pyc files).<br /> </li> <li>It is <span class="math inline">\(1 0 0 \%\)</span> free software.</li> </ul> </section> <section id="strengths" class="level1"> <h1>Strengths</h1> <ul> <li>It is quick to write, and there is no compilation.<br /> </li> <li>It is fully object oriented.<br /> </li> <li>It has many reliable libraries.<br /> </li> <li>It is an all-round language.</li> </ul> </section> <section id="weakness" class="level1"> <h1>Weakness</h1> <ul> <li>Writing very fast programs is not easy.</li> </ul> <section id="a.2.2-the-python-shell" class="level2"> <h2>A.2.2 The Python Shell</h2> <p>Overview</p> <p>You can use any Python command from the interactive Python shell <span class="math inline">\(\left( > > > \right)\)</span> :</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb511"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb511-1"><a href="#cb511-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> print 4**2 </span> <span id="cb511-2"><a href="#cb511-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>16 </span> <span id="cb511-3"><a href="#cb511-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> a = 'blue' </span> <span id="cb511-4"><a href="#cb511-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> print(a)</span> <span id="cb511-5"><a href="#cb511-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>blue </span> <span id="cb511-6"><a href="#cb511-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> </span></code></pre></div> </section> </section> <section id="tips" class="level1"> <h1>Tips</h1> <ul> <li>All Python commands work in the same way in the Python shell and in programs.<br /> </li> <li>You can leave the command line by Ctrl-D (Linux, Mac) or Ctrl-Z (Windows).<br /> </li> <li>The Python shell works great as a pocket calculator.<br /> </li> <li>Writing code blocks with more than two lines in the Python shell gets painful quickly.<br /> </li> <li>You can define code blocks by writing extra lines:</li> </ul> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb512"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb512-1"><a href="#cb512-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> for i in range(3): print(i, 012)</span> <span id="cb512-2"><a href="#cb512-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>> </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="a.2.3-python-programs" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.3 Python Programs</h1> <ul> <li>All program files should have the extension .py.<br /> </li> <li>Each line should contain exactly one command.<br /> </li> <li>Code blocks are marked by indentation. Code blocks should be indented by four spaces or one tab.<br /> </li> <li>When you are developing on UNIX, the first line in each Python program should be</li> </ul> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb513"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb513-1"><a href="#cb513-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>!/usr/bin/env python </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="code-formatting-conventions" class="level1"> <h1>Code Formatting Conventions</h1> <ul> <li>Use spaces instead of tabs (or use an editor that converts them automatically).<br /> </li> <li>Keep lines shorter than 80 characters long.<br /> </li> <li>Separate functions with two blank lines.<br /> </li> <li>Separate logical chunks of long functions with a single blank line.<br /> </li> <li>Variables and function names are in lowercase.</li> </ul> </section> <section id="the-dogma-of-programming" class="level1"> <h1>The Dogma of Programming</h1> <ul> <li>First, make it work.<br /> </li> <li>Second, make it nice.<br /> </li> <li>Third, and only if it is really necessary, make it fast.</li> </ul> </section> <section id="a.2.4-operators" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.4 Operators</h1> </section> <section id="arithmetic-operators" class="level1"> <h1>Arithmetic Operators</h1> <table> <tr> <td> 7 + 4 </td> <td> Addition; results in 11 (works for strings and lists) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7 - 4 </td> <td> Subtraction; results in 3 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7 * 4 </td> <td> Multiplication; results in 28 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7 / 4 </td> <td> Division of integers; results in 1 (rounded down) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7 / 4.0 </td> <td> Division by float; results in 1.75 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7 % 4 </td> <td> Modulo operator, returns the remainder of a division; results in 3 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7 ** 2 </td> <td> Raising to a power; results in 49 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 7.0 // 4.0 </td> <td> Floor division (cuts off after point); results in 1.0 </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="assignment-operators" class="level1"> <h1>Assignment Operators</h1> <p>Assignment operators create or modify variables.</p> <section id="variables" class="level4"> <h4>Variables</h4> <p>Variable are containers for data. Variable names may be composed of letters, underscores, and, after the first position, digits. Lowercase letters are common, but uppercase is also allowed (usually used for constants). Some words such as print, import, and for are forbidden as variable names.</p> <table> <tr> <td> a = 10 </td> <td> Assigns the integer value 10 to the variable a </td> </tr> <tr> <td> b = 3.0 </td> <td> Variable containing a floating-point number </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a3 = 10 </td> <td> Variable with a digit in its name </td> </tr> <tr> <td> PI = 3.1415 </td> <td> Variable written with uppercase letters </td> </tr> <tr> <td> invitation = 'Hello World' </td> <td> Variable containing text (string) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> invitation = "Hello World" </td> <td> Variable containing text with double quotes </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="modifying-variables" class="level4"> <h4>Modifying Variables</h4> <p><span class="math display">\[ + = , \quad - = , \quad \star = , \]</span></p> <p><span class="math display">\[ / = , \% = , \quad \star \star = \]</span></p> <p>Recursive operators</p> <p><span class="math display">\[ x + = 1 \text {i s e q u i v a l e n t} x = x + 1 \]</span></p> </section> </section> <section id="comparison-operators" class="level1"> <h1>Comparison Operators</h1> <p>All comparison variables result in either True or False.</p> <table> <tr> <td> a == b </td> <td> a equal to b </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a != b </td> <td> a not equal to b </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a < b </td> <td> a smaller than b </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a > b </td> <td> a bigger than b </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a <= b </td> <td> a smaller or equal to b </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a >= b </td> <td> a smaller or equal to b </td> </tr> <tr> <td> in, not in </td> <td> The in and not in operators check if the object on their left is contained (or not contained) in the string, list, or dictionary on their right and return the Boolean value True or False. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> is, is not </td> <td> The is and is not operators check whether the object on their left is identical (or not identical) to the object on their right and return the Boolean value True or False. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> a and b </td> <td> Boolean operator: if both the condition a and b are True, it returns True; else it returns False. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> or </td> <td> Boolean operator: if either the condition a or b or both are True, it returns True; else it returns False. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> not a </td> <td> Boolean operator: if the condition a is not verified, it returns True; else it returns False. </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="a.2.5-data-structures" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.5 Data Structures</h1> </section> <section id="overview-of-data-types" class="level1"> <h1>Overview of Data Types</h1> <table> <tr> <td> Integers </td> <td> Are numbers without digits after the decimal point </td> </tr> <tr> <td> Floats </td> <td> Are numbers with digits after the decimal point </td> </tr> <tr> <td> Strings </td> <td> Are immutable ordered collections of characters and are indicated with single ('abc') or double ("abc") quotation marks </td> </tr> <tr> <td> Lists </td> <td> Are mutable ordered collections of objects and are indicated with square brackets ([a, b, c]) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> Tuples </td> <td> Are immutable ordered collections of objects and are indicated with round brackets ((a, b, c)) or by listing the collection of items separated by commas (x, y, z) </td> </tr> <tr> <td> Dictionaries </td> <td> Are unordered collections of key: value pairs </td> </tr> <tr> <td> Sets </td> <td> Are collections of unique elements </td> </tr> <tr> <td> Boolean </td> <td> Are either True or False </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="type-conversions" class="level1"> <h1>Type Conversions</h1> <table> <tr> <td> int (value) </td> <td> Creates an integer from a float or string </td> </tr> <tr> <td> float (value) </td> <td> Creates a float from an int or string </td> </tr> <tr> <td> str(value) </td> <td> Creates a string from any variable </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="a.2.6-strings" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.6 Strings</h1> <p>String variables are containers for text. Strings can be marked by many kinds of quotes, which are all equivalent.</p> <table> <tr> <td> s = 'Hello World' </td> <td> Assigns the text to a string variable </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s = "Hello World" </td> <td> String with double quotes </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s = ['Hello World']'' </td> <td> Multiline string with triple single quotes </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s = ["Hello World"]'' </td> <td> Multiline string with triple double quotes </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s = 'Hello' </td> <td> String with a tab ( and a newline () </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="accessing-characters-and-substrings" class="level1"> <h1>Accessing Characters and Substrings</h1> <p>Using square brackets, any character of a string can be accessed by its position. The first character has the index 0. Substrings can be formed by square brackets with two numbers separated by a colon. The position corresponding to the second number is not included in the substring. If you try to use indices bigger than the length of the string, an IndexError will be created.</p> <table> <tr> <td> print s[0] </td> <td> Prints the first character </td> </tr> <tr> <td> print s[3] </td> <td> Prints the fourth character </td> </tr> <tr> <td> print s[-1] </td> <td> Prints the last character </td> </tr> <tr> <td> print s[1:4] </td> <td> Second to fourth position; results in 'ell' </td> </tr> <tr> <td> print s[:5] </td> <td> From start to fifth position; results in 'Hello' </td> </tr> <tr> <td> print s[-5:] </td> <td> Fifth position from the end until the end; results in 'World' </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="string-functions" class="level1"> <h1>String Functions</h1> <p>A number of functions can be used on every string variable:</p> <table> <tr> <td> len(s) </td> <td> Length of the string; results in 11 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> supper() </td> <td> Converts to uppercase; results in 'HELLO WORLD' </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s(lower() </td> <td> Converts to lowercase; results in 'hello world' </td> </tr> <tr> <td> strip() </td> <td> Removes spaces and tabs from both ends </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s.split('') </td> <td> Cuts into words; results in ['Hello', 'World'] </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s.find('llo') </td> <td> Searches for a substring; returns starting position </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s.replace('World', 'Moon') </td> <td> Replaces text; results in 'Hello Moon' </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s.startwith('Hello') </td> <td> Checks beginning; returns True or False </td> </tr> <tr> <td> s.endsWith('World') </td> <td> Checks end; returns True or False </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="a.2.7-lists" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.7 Lists</h1> <p>A list is a sequence of elements that can be modified. In many cases, all elements of a list will have the same type, but this is not mandatory.</p> </section> <section id="accessing-elements-of-lists" class="level1"> <h1>Accessing Elements of Lists</h1> <p>When you use square brackets, any element of a list can be accessed. The first character has the index 0.</p> <table> <tr> <td> data = [1,2,3,4,5] </td> <td> Creates a list </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[0] </td> <td> Accesses the first element </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[3] </td> <td> Accesses the fourth element </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[-1] </td> <td> Accesses the last element </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[0] = 7 </td> <td> Reassigns the first element </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="creating-lists-from-other-lists" class="level1"> <h1>Creating Lists from Other Lists</h1> <p>You can extract sublists from lists by applying square brackets in the same way as you would extract substrings.</p> <table> <tr> <td> data = [1,2,3,4,5] </td> <td> Creates a list </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[1:3] </td> <td> [2,3] </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[0:2] </td> <td> [1,2] </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[:3] </td> <td> [1,2,3] </td> </tr> <tr> <td> data[-2:] </td> <td> [4,5] </td> </tr> <tr> <td> backup = data[:] </td> <td> Creates a copy of the list </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="modifying-lists" class="level1"> <h1>Modifying Lists</h1> <table> <tr> <td> l[i] = x </td> <td> Theith element of l is replaced by x. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> l[i:j] = t </td> <td> The elements of l from i to j are replaced by t (iterable). </td> </tr> <tr> <td> del l[i:j] </td> <td> This deletes the elements of l from i to j. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> l[i:j:k] = t </td> <td> The elements l[i:j:k] are replaced by the elements of t (t must be a sequence such that len(l[i:j:k]) = len(t)). </td> </tr> <tr> <td> del s[i:j:k] </td> <td> This deletes the elements of l from i to j with step k. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.append(x) </td> <td> This is the same as l[len(1):len(1)] = [x].It appends the element x to the list 1. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1 extend(x) </td> <td> This is the same as l[len(1):len(1)] = x (where x is any iterable object). </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.count(x) </td> <td> This returns the number of elements x in 1. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.index(x[, i[, j]]) </td> <td> This returns the smaller k such that l[k] = x and i ≤ k ≤ j. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.insert(i, x) </td> <td> This is the same as l[i:i] = [x]. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.pop(i) </td> <td> This cancels theith element and returns its value. 1.pop() is the same as del l[-1]; return l[-1]. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.remove(x) </td> <td> This is the same as del l[1.index(x)]. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.reverse() </td> <td> This reverses the elements of l. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.sort() </td> <td> This sorts the list 1. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> 1.sort([[cmp[ key[, reverse]]]) </td> <td> This sorts the list 1. Optional arguments for the control of the comparison can be passed to the sort() method. cmp is a customized function for the comparison of element pairs that must return a negative value, zero, or a positive value depending on if the first element of the pair is lower than, equal to, or greater than the second element. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> sorted(1) </td> <td> This creates a new list made of a simple ascending sort of 1 (without modifying 1). </td> </tr> </table> <p>Functions Working on Lists</p> <pre class="csv"><code>data = [3,2,1,5] Example data len(data) Length of data; returns 4; also works for many other types min(data) Smallest element of data; returns 1 max(data) Biggest element of data; returns 5 sum(data) Sum of data; returns 11 range(4) Creates a list of numbers; returns [0,1,2,3] range(1,5) Creates a list with start value; returns [1,2,3,4] range(2,9,2) Creates a list with step size; returns [2,4,6,8] range(5,0,-1) Creates a list counting backward from the start value; returns [5,4,3,2,1] </code></pre> </section> <section id="a.2.8-tuples" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.8 Tuples</h1> <p>A tuple is a sequence of elements that cannot be modified. This means that once you have defined it, you cannot change or replace its elements. They are useful to group elements of different types.</p> <p><span class="math inline">\(t = ('\text{bananas}', '200g', 0.55)\)</span></p> <p>Notice that brackets are optional; i.e., you can use either Tuple <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> (1,2,3) or <span class="math inline">\(\mathtt { T u p l e } = 1 , 2 , 3\)</span> .</p> <p>A tuple of a single item must be written either Tuple = (1,) or Tuple <span class="math inline">\(= 1\)</span> .</p> <p>You can use square brackets to address elements of tuples in the same way as you would address elements of lists.</p> </section> <section id="a.2.9-dictionaries" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.9 Dictionaries</h1> <p>Dictionaries are an unordered, associative array. They have a set of key:value pairs:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb515"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb515-1"><a href="#cb515-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>prices = {'banana':0.75,'apple':0.55,'orange':0.80} </span></code></pre></div> <p>In the example, ‘banana’ is a key, and 0.75 is a value.</p> <p>Dictionaries can be used to look up things quickly:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb516"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb516-1"><a href="#cb516-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>prices['banana'] # 0.75 </span> <span id="cb516-2"><a href="#cb516-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>prices['kiwi'] # KeyError </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="accessing-data-in-dictionaries" class="level1"> <h1>Accessing Data in Dictionaries</h1> <p>By applying square brackets with a key inside, you can request the values of a dictionary. Keys can be strings, integers, floats, and tuples.</p> <table> <tr> <td> prices['banana'] </td> <td> This returns the value of 'banana' (0.75). </td> </tr> <tr> <td> prices.get('banana') </td> <td> This returns the value of 'banana' but avoids the KeyError. If the key does not exist, it returns None. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> prices.has_key('apple') </td> <td> This checks whether 'apple' is defined. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> prices.keys() </td> <td> This returns a list of all keys. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> prices.values() </td> <td> This returns a list of all values. </td> </tr> <tr> <td> prices.items() </td> <td> This returns all keys and values as a list of tuples. </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="modifying-dictionaries" class="level1"> <h1>Modifying Dictionaries</h1> <table> <tr> <td> prices['kiwi'] = 0.6 </td> <td> Sets the value of 'kiwi' </td> </tr> <tr> <td> prices.setdefault('egg',0.9) </td> <td> Sets the value of 'egg' if it is not defined yet </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="the-none-type" class="level1"> <h1>The None Type</h1> <p>Variables can contain the value None. You can also use None to indicate that a variable is empty. None is also used automatically when a function does not have a return statement.</p> <p>traffic_light <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> [None, None, ‘green’]</p> </section> <section id="a.2.10-control-flow" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.10 Control Flow</h1> </section> <section id="code-blocksindentation" class="level1"> <h1>Code Blocks/Indentation</h1> <p>After any statement ending with a colon (:), all indented commands are treated as a code block and are executed within the loop if if the condition applies. The next unindented command marks the end of the code block.</p> </section> <section id="loops-with-for" class="level1"> <h1>Loops with for</h1> <p>Loops repeat commands. They require a sequence of items that they iterate, e.g., a string, list, tuple, or dictionary. Lists are useful when you know the number of iterations in advance and when you want to do the same thing to all elements of a list.</p> <table> <tr> <td> for base in 'AGCT': print base </td> <td> Prints four lines containing A, G, C, and T </td> </tr> <tr> <td> for number in range(5): print number </td> <td> Prints five lines with the numbers from 0 to 4 </td> </tr> <tr> <td> for elem in [1, 4, 9, 16]: print elem </td> <td> Prints the four numbers each to a separate line </td> </tr> </table> </section> <section id="counting-through-elements-of-lists" class="level1"> <h1>Counting through Elements of Lists</h1> <p>The enumerate() function associates an integer number starting from zero to each element in a list. This is helpful in loops where an index variable is required.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb517"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb517-1"><a href="#cb517-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> fruits <span class="op">=</span> [<span class="st">'apple'</span>, <span class="st">'banana'</span>, <span class="st">'orange'</span>]</span> <span id="cb517-2"><a href="#cb517-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> <span class="cf">for</span> i, fruit <span class="kw">in</span> <span class="bu">enumerate</span>(fruits):</span> <span id="cb517-3"><a href="#cb517-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>... <span class="bu">print</span>(i, fruit)</span> <span id="cb517-4"><a href="#cb517-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>...</span> <span id="cb517-5"><a href="#cb517-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="dv">0</span> apple</span> <span id="cb517-6"><a href="#cb517-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="dv">1</span> banana</span> <span id="cb517-7"><a href="#cb517-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="dv">2</span> orange </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="merging-two-lists" class="level1"> <h1>Merging Two Lists</h1> <p>The zip() function associates the elements of two lists to a single list of tuples. Excess elements are ignored.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb518"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb518-1"><a href="#cb518-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> fruits <span class="op">=</span> [<span class="st">'apple'</span>, <span class="st">'banana'</span>, <span class="st">'orange'</span>]</span> <span id="cb518-2"><a href="#cb518-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> prices <span class="op">=</span> [<span class="fl">0.55</span>, <span class="fl">0.75</span>, <span class="fl">0.80</span>, <span class="fl">1.23</span>]</span> <span id="cb518-3"><a href="#cb518-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="op">>>></span> <span class="cf">for</span> fruit, price <span class="kw">in</span> <span class="bu">zip</span>(fruits, prices):</span> <span id="cb518-4"><a href="#cb518-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>...</span> <span id="cb518-5"><a href="#cb518-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="bu">print</span>(fruit, price)</span> <span id="cb518-6"><a href="#cb518-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>...</span> <span id="cb518-7"><a href="#cb518-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>apple <span class="fl">0.55</span></span> <span id="cb518-8"><a href="#cb518-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>banana <span class="fl">0.75</span></span> <span id="cb518-9"><a href="#cb518-9" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>orange <span class="fl">0.8</span> </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="loops-over-a-dictionary" class="level1"> <h1>Loops over a Dictionary</h1> <p>You can access the keys of a dictionary in a for loop. However, their order is not guaranteed.</p> </section> <section id="conditional-statements-with-if" class="level1"> <h1>Conditional Statements with if</h1> <p>if statements are used to implement decisions and branching in the program. They must contain an if block and optionally one or many elif and else blocks:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb519"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb519-1"><a href="#cb519-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">if</span> fruit <span class="op">==</span> <span class="st">'apple'</span>:</span> <span id="cb519-2"><a href="#cb519-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a> price <span class="op">=</span> <span class="fl">0.55</span></span> <span id="cb519-3"><a href="#cb519-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">elif</span> fruit <span class="op">==</span> <span class="st">'banana'</span>:</span> <span id="cb519-4"><a href="#cb519-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a> price <span class="op">=</span> <span class="fl">0.75</span></span> <span id="cb519-5"><a href="#cb519-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">elif</span> fruit <span class="op">==</span> <span class="st">'orange'</span>:</span> <span id="cb519-6"><a href="#cb519-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a> price <span class="op">=</span> <span class="fl">0.80</span></span> <span id="cb519-7"><a href="#cb519-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">else</span>:</span> <span id="cb519-8"><a href="#cb519-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a> <span class="bu">print</span>(<span class="st">'we dont have</span><span class="sc">%s</span><span class="st">'</span>)<span class="op">%</span>(fruit) </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="comparison-operators-1" class="level1"> <h1>Comparison Operators</h1> <p>An expression with if may contain any combination of comparison operators, variables, numbers, and function calls:</p> <ul> <li>a <span class="math inline">\(= = \mathrm { ~ b ~ }\)</span> , a != b (equality)<br /> </li> <li>a < b, a > b, a <= b, a >= b (relations)<br /> </li> <li>a or b, a and b, not a (Boolean logic)<br /> </li> <li>(a or b) and (c or d) (priority)<br /> </li> <li>a in b (inclusion, when b is a list, tuple, or string)</li> </ul> <section id="boolean-value-of-variables" class="level4"> <h4>Boolean Value of Variables</h4> <p>Apart from the comparison operators, the if statement also takes the values of variables directly into account. Each variable can be interpreted by Boolean logic. All variables are True, except for</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb520"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb520-1"><a href="#cb520-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>0, 0.0, '', [], {}, False, None </span></code></pre></div> </section> </section> <section id="conditional-loops-with-while" class="level1"> <h1>Conditional Loops with while</h1> <p>while loops require a conditional expression at the beginning. These work in exactly the same way as in if…elif statements:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb521"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb521-1"><a href="#cb521-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>>i=0 </span> <span id="cb521-2"><a href="#cb521-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>>>>while i<5:printi, </span> <span id="cb521-3"><a href="#cb521-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>... </span> <span id="cb521-4"><a href="#cb521-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>i=i+1 </span> <span id="cb521-5"><a href="#cb521-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>01234 </span></code></pre></div> <p>When to Use while</p> <ul> <li>When there is a loop exit condition<br /> </li> <li>When you want to start a loop only upon a given condition<br /> </li> <li>When it may happen that nothing is done at all<br /> </li> <li>When the number of repeats depends on user input<br /> </li> <li>When you are searching for a particular element in a list</li> </ul> </section> <section id="a.2.11-program-structures" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.11 Program Structures</h1> </section> <section id="functions" class="level1"> <h1>Functions</h1> <p>Functions are subprograms. They help you to structure your code into logical units. A function may have its own variables. It also has an input (parameters) and output (returned values). In Python, a function is defined by the def statement, followed by the function name, the argument(s) in brackets, a colon (:), and an indented code block:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb522"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb522-1"><a href="#cb522-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="kw">def</span> calc_discount(fruit, n):</span> <span id="cb522-2"><a href="#cb522-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a> <span class="co">'''Returns a lower price of a fruit.'' print('Today we have a special offer for: '), fruit return 0.75 * n</span></span> <span id="cb522-3"><a href="#cb522-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="co">print calc_discount('banana', 10) </span></span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="parameters-and-return-values" class="level1"> <h1>Parameters and Return Values</h1> <p>Input for a function is given by arguments. Arguments may have default values. Then they are optional in the function call. Do not use lists or dictionaries as default values!</p> <p>The output of a function is created by the return statement. The value given to return goes to the program part that called it. More than one value is returned as a tuple. In any case, the return statement ends the function execution.</p> <p>def calc_disc(fruit, n = 1): # A function with an optional print fruit # parameter return n*0.75<br /> def calc_disc(fruit, n = 1): # A function returning a tuple fruit <span class="math inline">\(=\)</span> fruit-upper() return fruit, n*0.75<br /> calc_disc(‘banana’) # Function calls<br /> calc_disc(‘banana’,100)</p> </section> <section id="good-style-for-writing-functions" class="level1"> <h1>Good Style for Writing Functions</h1> <ul> <li>Each function should have one purpose only.<br /> </li> <li>The name should be clear and start with a verb.<br /> </li> <li>The function should have a triple-quoted comment at the beginning (a documentation string).<br /> </li> <li>The function should return results in only one way.<br /> </li> <li>Functions should be small (fewer than 100 lines).</li> </ul> </section> <section id="a.2.12-modules" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.12 Modules</h1> <p>A module is a Python file (the filename ending with .py). Modules can be imported from another Python program. When a module is imported, the code within is automatically executed. To import from a module, you need to give its name (without .py) in the import statement. It is helpful to explicitly list the variables and functions required. This helps with debugging.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb523"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb523-1"><a href="#cb523-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> math</span> <span id="cb523-2"><a href="#cb523-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> sqrt</span> <span id="cb523-3"><a href="#cb523-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> pi</span> <span id="cb523-4"><a href="#cb523-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> sqrt, pi</span> <span id="cb523-5"><a href="#cb523-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> <span class="op">*</span></span> <span id="cb523-6"><a href="#cb523-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">from</span> math <span class="im">import</span> <span class="op">*</span> </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="finding-out-what-is-in-a-module" class="level1"> <h1>Finding Out What Is in a Module</h1> <p>The contents of any module can be examined with dir() and help().</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb524"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb524-1"><a href="#cb524-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>dir(math) Shows everything inside the module </span> <span id="cb524-2"><a href="#cb524-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>dir() Shows everything in the global namespace </span> <span id="cb524-3"><a href="#cb524-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>help(math.sqrt) Displays the help text of a module or function </span> <span id="cb524-4"><a href="#cb524-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>__name__ Name of a module </span> <span id="cb524-5"><a href="#cb524-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>__doc__ Help text of a module </span> <span id="cb524-6"><a href="#cb524-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>__builtin__ Container with all standard Python functions </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="where-python-looks-for-modules" class="level1"> <h1>Where Python Looks for Modules</h1> <p>When importing modules or packages, Python looks in</p> <ul> <li><p>the current directory,<br /> </p></li> <li><p>the Python2.6/lib/site-packages folder, and</p></li> <li><p>everything in the PYTHONPATH environment variable. In Python, it can be accessed with</p></li> </ul> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb525"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb525-1"><a href="#cb525-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> sys </span> <span id="cb525-2"><a href="#cb525-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="bu">print</span> sys.path </span> <span id="cb525-3"><a href="#cb525-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>sys.path.append(<span class="st">'my_directory'</span>) </span> <span id="cb525-4"><a href="#cb525-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="im">import</span> mypackage.my_module </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="packages" class="level1"> <h1>Packages</h1> <p>For very big programs, you might find it useful to divide the Python code into several directories. There are two things to keep in mind when doing that:</p> <ul> <li>To import the package from outside, you need to ensure a file __ init__.py (it may be empty) is in the package directory.<br /> </li> <li>The directory with the package needs to be in the Python search path (see above).</li> </ul> </section> <section id="a.2.13-input-and-output" class="level1"> <h1>A.2.13 Input and Output</h1> <p>Reading Text from the Keyboard into a Variable</p> <p>User input can be read from the keyboard with or without a message text:</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb526"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb526-1"><a href="#cb526-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>a <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">raw_input</span>() Reads text <span class="im">from</span> the keyboard to a string variable </span> <span id="cb526-2"><a href="#cb526-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>a <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">raw_input</span>(<span class="st">'please Enter a number'</span>) Displays the text, then reads a string <span class="im">from</span> the keyboard </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="printing-text" class="level1"> <h1>Printing Text</h1> <p>The Python print statement writes text to the console where Python was started. The print command is very versatile and accepts almost any combination of strings, numbers, function calls, and arithmetic operations separated by commas. By default, print generates a newline character at the end.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb527"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb527-1"><a href="#cb527-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('Hello World') Displays a text </span> <span id="cb527-2"><a href="#cb527-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print 3.4 Displays the number </span> <span id="cb527-3"><a href="#cb527-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print 3 + 4 Displays the result of the calculation </span> <span id="cb527-4"><a href="#cb527-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print a Displays the contents of the variable a </span> <span id="cb527-5"><a href="#cb527-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('')'line one line two line three''' Displays text stretching over multiple lines </span> <span id="cb527-6"><a href="#cb527-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('number'), 77 Displays the text, a tab, and the number </span></code></pre></div> <p>print int(a) * 7</p> <p>print</p> <p>Displays the result of the multiplication after converting the variable to an integer</p> <p>Displays an empty line</p> </section> <section id="string-formatting" class="level1"> <h1>String Formatting</h1> <p>Variables and strings can be combined using formatting characters. This works also within a print statement. In both cases, the number of values and formatting characters must be equal.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb528"><pre class="sourceCode txt"><code class="sourceCode default"><span id="cb528-1"><a href="#cb528-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>s = 'Result:%i%' (number) </span> <span id="cb528-2"><a href="#cb528-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('Hello%s!') ('Roger') </span> <span id="cb528-3"><a href="#cb528-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>print('(6.3f/%6.3f)')%(a,b) </span></code></pre></div> <p>The formatting characters include the following:</p> <ul> <li>%i: an integer<br /> </li> <li>%4i: an integer formatted to length 4<br /> </li> <li>%6.2f: a float number with length 6 and 2 after the comma<br /> </li> <li>%10s: a right-oriented string with length 10</li> </ul> </section> <section id="reading-and-writing-files" class="level1"> <h1>Reading and Writing Files</h1> <p>Text files can be accessed using the open() function. It returns an open file whose contents can be extracted as a string or strings that can be written. If you try to open a file that does not exist, an IOError will be created.</p> <div class="sourceCode" id="cb529"><pre class="sourceCode python"><code class="sourceCode python"><span id="cb529-1"><a href="#cb529-1" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>) Reads a text <span class="bu">file</span> <span class="kw">and</span> its contents into a </span> <span id="cb529-2"><a href="#cb529-2" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>text <span class="op">=</span> f.read() string variable </span> <span id="cb529-3"><a href="#cb529-3" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>, <span class="st">'w'</span>) Creates a new text <span class="bu">file</span> <span class="kw">and</span> writes text <span class="im">from</span> </span> <span id="cb529-4"><a href="#cb529-4" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.write(text) a string variable into it </span> <span id="cb529-5"><a href="#cb529-5" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f <span class="op">=</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>, <span class="st">'a'</span>) Appends text to an already existing <span class="bu">file</span> </span> <span id="cb529-6"><a href="#cb529-6" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.write(text) </span> <span id="cb529-7"><a href="#cb529-7" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.close() Closes a <span class="bu">file</span> after usage<span class="op">;</span> closing <span class="kw">in</span> Python </span> <span id="cb529-8"><a href="#cb529-8" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>lines <span class="op">=</span> f.readlines() <span class="kw">is</span> good style but <span class="kw">not</span> always mandatory </span> <span id="cb529-9"><a href="#cb529-9" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.writelines lines) Reads <span class="bu">all</span> lines <span class="im">from</span> a text <span class="bu">file</span> to a <span class="bu">list</span> </span> <span id="cb529-10"><a href="#cb529-10" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="cf">for</span> line <span class="kw">in</span> <span class="bu">open</span>(name): Goes through <span class="bu">all</span> lines <span class="kw">and</span> prints each line </span> <span id="cb529-11"><a href="#cb529-11" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="bu">print</span>(line)</span> <span id="cb529-12"><a href="#cb529-12" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>lines <span class="op">=</span> [<span class="st">'first line</span><span class="ch">\n</span><span class="st">'</span>, <span class="st">'second'</span> creates a <span class="bu">list</span> of lines <span class="cf">with</span> newline </span> <span id="cb529-13"><a href="#cb529-13" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>line\n<span class="st">'] characters at the end and saves it to a </span></span> <span id="cb529-14"><a href="#cb529-14" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a><span class="er">f = open</span>(<span class="st">'my_file.txt'</span>, <span class="st">'w'</span>) text <span class="bu">file</span> </span> <span id="cb529-15"><a href="#cb529-15" aria-hidden="true" tabindex="-1"></a>f.writelines (lines) </span></code></pre></div> </section> <section id="directory-names-in-python" class="level1"> <h1>Directory Names in Python</h1> <p>When opening files, you can also use full or relative directory names.
